This review consolidates recent advancements, systematically classifying progress based on interfacial configurations in S–S and S–L systems and the underlying charge transfer dynamics. To unlock the full potential of CE-Chemistry, future research should prioritize the strategic tuning of material electronegativity, the engineering of sophisticated surface architectures, and the enhancement of charge transport mechanisms, paving the way for sustainable chemical innovations.

results.title: Revisiting YSZ Capacitance: Experimental Challenges and Reliable Measurement Strategies
results.citation: M. Carda et al. (2025) Revisiting YSZ Capacitance: Experimental Challenges and Reliable Measurement Strategies doi:10.1149/ma2025-031369mtgabs
results.abstract:
Yttria-stabilized zirconia (YSZ) is the most commonly used electrolyte material in solid oxide cells due to its high oxygen ion conductivity, chemical and mechanical stability at elevated temperatures. A fundamental parameter influencing its electrochemical behavior is its capacitance, which plays a key role in impedance spectroscopy analysis, near surface space-charge modeling (i.e., the nature of electric double-layer - EDL), and the interpretation of interfacial phenomena in solid oxide cell architectures. Despite the widespread use of capacitance data in electrochemical models, the literature reports a strikingly broad range of values making their direct implementation in modeling at operational conditions unreliable. [1-3] This is in a stark contrast to its importance as the understanding of EDL is vital for the explanation of phenomena, such as charge accumulation and ion transport. In addition, the magnitude of double-layer capacitance is one of the few experimentally accessible equilibrium properties of a material, thus it is essential for mathematical modeling.
 Although the electric double-layer is a well-established concept in electrochemistry, its accurate determination in solid oxide cells is particularly challenging due to the non-ideal nature of the system. The double-layer capacitance is governed by the physical and chemical properties of both the electrode and the electrolyte, with charge accumulation at the metal electrode-YSZ electrolyte interface arising from two primary mechanisms: (a) the formation of intrinsic dielectric response of the electrolyte (the double layer itself), and (b) time-dependent faradaic processes, known as pseudocapacitance. These interfacial effects can lead to an overestimation of the true bulk capacitance of YSZ, thus further complicating the interpretation of experimental results. [4] This latter mechanism can be one of the main sources of discrepancies in reported capacitance values due to the frequent utilization of experimental setups that introduce pseudocapacitance at the YSZ electrolyte-electrode interface. [5] Another significant factor contributing to inconsistencies is the wide range of experimental temperatures used in different studies. Below 400 °C, the ionic conductivity of YSZ is negligible, which can reduce the impact of pseudocapacitance in measurements. However, at such low temperatures, the resulting YSZ bulk capacitance may significantly deviate from the values measured at operational temperatures (600–800 °C). At these temperatures, in addition to the occurrence of pseudocapacitance, dielectric contributions from grain boundaries, electrode interfaces, and residual space-charge effects become more significant, thus further complicating the interpretation of experimental data. Given that electrochemical models [6] for solid oxide cells require accurate input parameters to predict performance and degradation mechanisms, this lack of consistency in reported capacitance values represents a fundamental challenge.
 In this study, we systematically investigate the capacitance of YSZ by employing three distinct experimental setups to determine the conditions under which more reliable data can be obtained. By critically analyzing the discrepancies between the obtained results, we aim to contribute to the understanding of the impact of experimental design on capacitance measurements and assess the influence of crystallographic orientation. This work provides insights into factors affecting capacitance determination in YSZ, which can help refine electrochemical modeling and improve the reliability of capacitance data for solid oxide cell applications.
 
 Commercially available monocrystalline YSZ wafers (BioTain Crystal Co., Ltd., 1 mm thickness, 2.5 cm
 2
 ) containing 13 mol.% of Y
 2
 O
 3
 with <100>, <110>, and <111> plane orientations were used as electrolytes. Electrodes were symmetrically applied onto both sides of the electrolyte using either Au (fuelcellmaterials.com) or Ag (in-house prepared) ink, ensuring precise alignment and electrode geometry. The cells were calcined at 950 °C for 3 h for Au electrodes and at 800 °C for 2 h for Ag electrodes to ensure proper adhesion and electrode stability.
 
 To investigate the impact of pseudocapacitance on the measured capacitance values, three distinct experimental setups were utilized. The first configuration ('Cell 1') used Ag electrodes exposed to air and served as a reference system. The second setup ('Cell 2') incorporated a Hebb-Wagner configuration, in which one electrode was sealed from air using a glass barrier to suppress its electrochemical activity. The final configuration ('Cell 3') employed Au electrodes exposed to air.
 
 Measurements were conducted in air atmosphere over a temperature range of 500–800 °C. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and applied bias experiments were performed using a Solartron SI 1287 electrochemical interface and Solartron SI 1260 frequency response analyzer (Ametek/Solartron Metrology, UK). For the Hebb-Wagner setup ('Cell 2'), EIS measurements were conducted under a controlled bias of 0.3 V vs. CE to maintain cell integrity by mitigating oxygen accumulation at the blocked electrode. Prior to measurement, the sample was stabilized at this bias for 10 min, followed by EIS spectra acquisition (perturbing signal: 50 mV; frequency range: 65 kHz–0.1 Hz). The setups with exposed electrodes ('Cell 1' and 'Cell 3') were characterized under open-circuit voltage (OCV) conditions, followed by potentiostatic measurements with voltage steps from 0 V to ±1 V in 0.05 V increments. Each step was held for 2 min before EIS spectra were recorded using identical parameters. At the end of each experiment, cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed from 0 V to ±1 V vs. CE at a scan rate of 5 mV s
 -1
 .
 
 
 The setup with Ag electrodes exposed to air (‘Cell 1’) served as a reference system due to the electrochemical activity of Ag towards oxygen evolution and reduction reactions (OER/ORR). The measurements revealed that the true bulk capacitance of YSZ was obscured by a dominant pseudocapacitive contribution, which was several orders of magnitude larger than the expected capacitance. The obtained capacitance values were in the range of 1–2 F m
 -2
 at 800 °C, independent of the crystallographic plane. Although these values are comparable to those reported in previous studies, attributing such large capacitance solely to the charging of the electric double layer is unrealistic.
 
 
 To mitigate faradaic contributions, a Hebb-Wagner cell was employed by sealing one electrode with a glass barrier to block oxygen access (‘Cell 2’). Despite this design and the precaution of pre-polarization (as detailed in the experimental section), the results did not meet expectations. Residual oxygen access to the supposedly blocking electrode led to continued faradaic processes, rendering the setup unsuitable for measuring the intrinsic bulk capacitance of YSZ. The observed capacitance values remained similar to those from Cell 1 (~1 F m
 -2
 at 800 °C), indicating that pseudocapacitance was still a dominant factor.
 
 
 The most reliable results were obtained with Au electrodes (‘Cell 3’), as gold exhibits negligible catalytic activity towards OER/ORR. This ensured that both electrodes behaved irreversibly, significantly reducing the faradaic contribution. Although a minor residual faradaic current was observed (~6·10
 -4
 A cm
 2
 at 800 °C and +1 V vs CE), its impact on the capacitance measurements was negligible. The samples exhibited three distinct capacitance peaks. Beyond ±0.5 V, the I–V curves displayed non-zero steady-state currents, indicating an increase in faradaic (pseudo)capacitance with reaction rate. The formation of an adsorbed oxygen layer likely contributed to higher capacitance values towards the end of the scan cycle. The capacitance values for all crystallographic planes and across the entire experimental temperature range were found to be between 0.06–0.25 F·m⁻².
 
 The results of this study highlight the importance of experimental design in accurately determining the capacitance of YSZ. Among the three investigated setups, only the cell with Au electrodes provided reliable capacitance values, as it minimized the influence of pseudocapacitance. The obtained capacitance values fall within a reasonable range, aligning with theoretical expectations. While some variability was observed among different crystallographic planes, these differences were minor and likely attributable to experimental uncertainty. Overall, this study contributes to a more precise determination of YSZ capacitance and underscores the necessity of proper electrode selection and measurement conditions to obtain meaningful electrochemical data.
 This work was supported by the Technology Agency of the Czech Republic under project no. TK04030143 (Advanced reversible system for hydrogen production on base of the high temperature solid oxide cell).
 
 [1] X. Vendrell, et al., Electrical properties of yttria-stabilized zirconia, YSZ single crystal: Local AC and long range DC conduction.
 J. Electrochem. Soc.
 2018
 ,
 165
 , F966-F975,
 https://dx.doi.org/10.1149/2.0881811jes
 .
 
 
 [2] J. Nielsen, et al., Impedance of SOFC electrodes: A review and a comprehensive case study on the impedance of LSM:YSZ cathodes.
 Electrochim. Acta
 2014
 ,
 115
 , 31-45,
 https://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2013.10.053
 .
 
 
 [3] M. G. H. M. Hendriks, et al., The electrochemical double-layer capacitance of yttria-stabilised zirconia.
 Solid State Ionics
 2002
 ,
 146
 , 211-217,
 https://dx.doi.org/10.1016/S0167-2738(01)01017-7
 .
 
 
 [4] N. L. Robertson, et al., Double Layer Capacitance of Porous Platinum Electrodes in Zirconia Electrochemical Cells.
 J. Electrochem. Soc.
 1991
 ,
 138
 , 1494-1499,
 https://dx.doi.org/10.1149/1.2085814
 .
 
 
 [5] G. Fóti, et al., Charge storage at the Pt/YSZ interface.

results.title: Magnetic properties of molecules on surfaces studied with scanning probe methods
results.citation: Chao Li et al. (2026) Magnetic properties of molecules on surfaces studied with scanning probe methods doi:10.1093/nsr/nwag109
results.abstract: ABSTRACT Magnetic molecules on surfaces provide atomically precise test beds for quantum magnetism and device concepts in spintronics and quantum information. Scanning probe techniques now resolve and control single-molecule spin states, their coupling to substrates and emergent many-body phenomena, including the Kondo effect and Yu–Shiba–Rusinov states. This review summarizes recent progress in combining molecular design, adsorption geometry and interfacial hybridization to create measurable spin anisotropy, excitations and correlations. We first recall the fundamentals of spin formation and surface-induced modifications, then survey key methodologies such as scanning tunneling microscopy, inelastic tunneling spectroscopy, pump–probe protocols, functionalized and superconducting tips, and magnetic exchange force microscopy. Case studies span single-ion magnets, metal-free Kondo-active molecules, molecular magnets on superconductors, and coupled spin architectures assembled by on-surface synthesis. Across these examples, we distill design rules for tuning exchange, anisotropy and charge transfer at the molecule–surface interface. We conclude with open challenges—reproducibility, room-temperature stability and multiscale theory—and outline opportunities in van der Waals integration, ultrafast spin dynamics and data-driven spectroscopy, charting a path from single-molecule experiments to programmable molecular spin devices.

results.title: Weakly-Solvating Electrolyte Enabling Enhanced Ultra-Low Temperature Performance for Silicon-Based Anodes
results.citation: Esin Aydemir et al. (2025) Weakly-Solvating Electrolyte Enabling Enhanced Ultra-Low Temperature Performance for Silicon-Based Anodes doi:10.1149/ma2025-022199mtgabs
results.abstract:
The expanding market for electric vehicles (EVs) and increasing demand for reliable grid-scale energy storage systems necessitate batteries with a high energy density
 1
 . The specific energy of the state-of-the-art lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) with graphite (Gr) anode (~250 Wh/kg) is not sufficient to meet the high energy density requirement of next generation LIBs (>350 Wh/kg).
 1,2
 Since the atomic framework of Gr limits the extent of the intercalation reaction with Li
 +
 , significant effort has been dedicated to searching for alternative anode chemistries delivering higher specific theoretical capacities than that of Gr (372 mAh/g).
 3
 Therefore, silicon (Si) is being considered as a promising substitute for Gr due to its high theoretical specific capacity of >3500 mAh/g.
 1
 However, Si undergoes drastic volumetric changes after full lithiation (~300%), which results in excessive particle fracture and electrical isolation in addition to an unstable solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) formation, leading to a fast capacity decay.
 3
 The SEI layer is recognized to play a substantial role in mitigating parasitic reactions with the electrolyte and stabilizing the Si anode interphase.
 4
 Efforts have been made to design electrolytes yielding a robust SEI layer, optimal ionic conductivity, and fast charge-transfer kinetics simultaneously, inducing a stable cycling performance in a wide temperature range.
 5
 
 
 Prior work to address these challenges include the design of Li
 +
 solvation shell, as it plays a deterministic role on the SEI chemistry.
 6
 Although increasing the salt-to-solvent ratio has been adopted as one strategy to obtain an anion-rich, inorganic interphase, highly concentrated electrolytes (HCEs) suffer from high viscosity and poor wetting of the electrode and the separator, stimulating the search for alternative strategies enabling anion-derived interphase and good ionic conductivity at moderate salt concentrations.
 7
 In this respect, weakly-solvating electrolytes (WSEs) maintain a higher degree of ion aggregates at low salt concentrations (< 3M) due to the reduced intrinsic solvation power of solvent molecules.
 7
 Nonetheless, weakened Li
 +
 -solvent coordination in WSEs might result in low ionic conductivity and sluggish Li
 +
 transport, compromising the performance at LT.
 6
 
 
 In this work, we investigate the interfacial and bulk properties of three different dilute ether-based electrolytes and how those properties are influenced by the solvation structure. Detailed analyses of the SEI and physicochemical properties of the electrolytes demonstrate that the partially fluorinated ether delivers a more stable cycling performance down to -60 °C as well as the highest ionic conductivity and the lowest viscosity. This is achieved by the synergistic effect of the decreased desolvation-energy barrier and improved ionic conductivity, which can be attributed to the comprehensive design of the anion-rich solvation structure. The findings of this research are valuable in terms of demonstrating how the modification of the chelating sites and the solvent structure can impact the charge-transfer kinetics, SEI, and bulk Li
 +
 transport. We believe that our results may prove to be a helpful point of reference for further research in the field of electrolyte engineering.

 et al.

 , 3586–3594 (2023).

 et al.

 Nat. Commun.
 12
 , 5459 (2021).

 Adv. Mater.

 , 4966–4985 (2013).

 et al.

 Nat. Energy
 5
 , 386–397 (2020).

 et al.

 Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.

 , e202218151 (2023).

 , 1–26 (2018).

results.title: (Invited) Interfacial Engineering of Battery Materials Using Atomic Layer Deposition
results.citation: N. Dasgupta (2022) (Invited) Interfacial Engineering of Battery Materials Using Atomic Layer Deposition doi:10.1149/ma2022-02471732mtgabs
results.abstract:
In batteries, like all electrochemical systems, interfacial phenomena play a critical role in thermodynamic stability, charge transfer kinetics, and ionic transport phenomena. For example, in Li-ion batteries, the solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer enables stabilization of liquid electrolytes against continual electrolyte decomposition. In solid-state batteries, the dynamic morphological evolution of the interface results in unique electro-chemo-mechanical properties, which dictate performance, degradation, and ultimate failure.
 As a result of these coupled phenomena, there is a continued need to improve both our fundamental understanding of interfacial dynamics, as well as new strategies to engineer rationally-controlled interfaces. Among the various interfacial engineering methodologies, atomic layer deposition (ALD) has risen in interest in recent years, owing to its unparalleled ability to impart atomically-precise control of surface composition and structure, while maintaining conformal coating of ultra-high aspect ratio structures.
 In this talk, I will describe recent progress in applying ALD for battery applications. In the field of Li-ion batteries, I will show how deposition of a single-ion conducting solid electrolyte on the surface of graphite anodes can eliminate electrolyte decomposition and SEI formation, resulting in a four-fold decrease in interfacial resistance [1]. This results in lower cell polarization during fast charging, eliminating deleterious Li plating on the anode. In the field of Li metal batteries, I will also show how ALD coatings can influence nucleation and growth, resulting in improved Coulombic efficiency [2]. Finally, I will describe efforts towards incorporation of ALD films into solid-state batteries, where mechanical properties play a critical role [3-4].
 [1] E. Kazyak, K.-H. Chen, Y. Chen, T. H. Cho, N. P. Dasgupta, Adv. Energy Mater. 
 12, 2102618 (2022).
 [2] K.-H. Chen, A. J. Sanchez, E. Kazyak, A. L. Davis, N. P. Dasgupta, Adv. Energy Mater. 
 9, 1802534 (2019).
 [3] A. L. Davis, R. Garcia-Mendez, K. N. Wood, E. Kazyak, K.-H. Chen, G. Teeter, J. Sakamoto, N. P. Dasgupta, 
 J. Mater. Chem. A 
 8, 6291 (2020)
 [4] E. Kazyak, M. Shin, W. S. LePage, T. H. Cho, N. P. Dasgupta, 
 Chem. Commun. 
 56, 15537 (2020).

results.title: Interfaces Comprehension at Electrodes on Sulfide-Based Solid State Batteries
results.citation: A. Orue et al. (2024) Interfaces Comprehension at Electrodes on Sulfide-Based Solid State Batteries doi:10.1149/ma2024-012220mtgabs
results.abstract:
The increasing demand for safe, highly efficient, and cost-effective energy storage systems has accelerated the development of solid-state batteries (SSBs) with lithium metal (LiM) anodes. This technology offers remarkable advantages over conventional lithium-ion batteries with liquid electrolytes; from improved safety with non-flammable electrolyte to higher gravimetric and volumetric energy density enabled using LiM anode along with the multilayered bipolar stacking cell fabrication. The combination of solid electrolyte (SE) mechanical strength, flexibility, and safety against self-ignition allows for optimized battery design to meet the specific requirements.1–4
 
 In recent years, soft, flexible, and easily deformable sulfide-based solid electrolytes have received significant attention due to their attractive mechanical properties, high conductivity, and feasibility of processing at ambient temperature, which makes them appealing candidates for upscaling the technology to large format devices.2,4,5 Despite the high ionic conductivity and attractive mechanical properties of sulfide-based SSBs, this chemistry still faces key challenges to encompass fast rate and long cycling performance, mainly arising from the dynamic and complex solid-solid interfaces. Solid-state materials are characterized by a significant impact of interface-related phenomena on their functional characteristics, such as mechanical properties, conductivity mechanisms, or electrochemical performance. For SSBs with ceramic or glass-ceramic electrolytes, the stacking of the composite cathode, the SE and the LiM anode leads to multiple interfaces from voids, cracks, secondary phases from chemical and electrochemical reactions and grain boundaries.6,7 As electrochemical kinetics is governed by the contact area at the interface, ensuring the adhesion between the electrolyte and active electrode materials is the key to obtain high performance devices, especially at fast rates.
 The goal of the present work is to provide understanding on the complex solid-solid interfaces regulating sulfide-based battery performance. As shown in Figure 1, cells under different configurations with LPSCl, Li or In metal anodes, and LiNi0.6Mn0.2Co0.2O2 (NMC622)-based composite cathodes have been systematically studied aiming to bring insights on the dynamic interfaces at component level dominating processes during cell operation. From a multi-configurational approach and an advanced deconvolution of electrochemical impedance signals into distribution of relaxation times, we disentangle intricate underlying interfacial processes taking place at the battery components and that play major role on the overall performance. For the Li metal solid-state batteries, the cycling performance is highly sensitive to the chemomechanical properties of the cathode active material, the formation of the SEI, and processes ascribed to Li diffusion in the cathode composite and in the space-charge layer. The outcomes of this work aim to facilitate the design of sulfide solid-state batteries and provide methodological inputs for battery ageing assessment.
 (1) Zheng, F.; Kotobuki, M.; Song, S.; Lai, M. O.; Lu, L. Review on Solid Electrolytes for All-Solid-State Lithium-Ion Batteries. Journal of Power Sources
 2018, 389, 198–213. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpowsour.2018.04.022.
 (2) Randau, S.; Weber, D. A.; Kötz, O.; Koerver, R.; Braun, P.; Weber, A.; Ivers-Tiffée, E.; Adermann, T.; Kulisch, J.; Zeier, W. G.; Richter, F. H.; Janek, J. Benchmarking the Performance of All-Solid-State Lithium Batteries. Nat Energy
 2020, 5 (3), 259–270. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41560-020-0565-1.
 (3) Schnell, J.; Günther, T.; Knoche, T.; Vieider, C.; Köhler, L.; Just, A.; Keller, M.; Passerini, S.; Reinhart, G. All-Solid-State Lithium-Ion and Lithium Metal Batteries – Paving the Way to Large-Scale Production. Journal of Power Sources
 2018, 382, 160–175. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpowsour.2018.02.062.
 (4) Kim, K. J.; Balaish, M.; Wadaguchi, M.; Kong, L.; Rupp, J. L. M. Solid‐State Li–Metal Batteries: Challenges and Horizons of Oxide and Sulfide Solid Electrolytes and Their Interfaces. Advanced Energy Materials
 2021, 11 (1), 2002689. https://doi.org/10.1002/aenm.202002689.
 (5) Liu, S.; Zhou, L.; Han, J.; Wen, K.; Guan, S.; Xue, C.; Zhang, Z.; Xu, B.; Lin, Y.; Shen, Y.; Li, L.; Nan, C. Super Long‐Cycling All‐Solid‐State Battery with Thin Li 6 PS 5 Cl‐Based Electrolyte. Advanced Energy Materials
 2022, 12 (25), 2200660. https://doi.org/10.1002/aenm.202200660.
 (6) Pesci, F. M.; Bertei, A.; Brugge, R. H.; Emge, S. P.; Hekselman, A. K. O.; Marbella, L. E.; Grey, C. P.; Aguadero, A. Establishing Ultralow Activation Energies for Lithium Transport in Garnet Electrolytes. ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces
 2020, 12 (29), 32806–32816. https://doi.org/10.1021/acsami.0c08605.
 (7) Chen, R.; Li, Q.; Yu, X.; Chen, L.; Li, H. Approaching Practically Accessible Solid-State Batteries: Stability Issues Related to Solid Electrolytes and Interfaces. Chem. Rev.
 2020, 120 (14), 6820–6877. https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.chemrev.9b00268.

results.title: Evaluation of Relationship Between Dynamic Structural Deformation and Output Performance in Lithium-Ion Battery By Numerical Simulation
results.citation: Gen Inoue (2015) Evaluation of Relationship Between Dynamic Structural Deformation and Output Performance in Lithium-Ion Battery By Numerical Simulation doi:10.1149/ma2015-02/2/164
results.abstract:
In order to improve the performance of Lithium ion secondary battery (LiB), it is very important to understanding the internal transport phenomena under high rate condition to increase power density. In my previous study, I focused on the actual porous electrode structure, and the Li ion and electron conductivity was evaluated by the effective conductive path with the actual reconstruction electrode structure [1], [2]. However, the effect of volume expansion of charge-discharge state is not been understood enough. In this study, I examined the new model with structure change based on the actual porous electrode structure, and the relationship between structural change and battery performance was considered. Actual Graphite anode and LiCoO2 cathode electrode structure were reconstructed by FIB-SEM, and the reaction and mass transfer in these reconstructed electrode structure and simulated separator structure were simulated by multi-block method [3] which is the method of coarse graining to keep the information of heterogeneous structure. Li concentration and potential distribution in active material (AM) and electrolyte (EL) were calculated with Butler-Volmer equation as the boundary condition of the interface between AM and EL. it was assumed that temperature is constant, the formation of SEI is ignored, binder phase inhibit ionic transfer. The discharge performance was calculated with 1M LiPF6－EC/DEC (1:1) and the condition reported previous research[4]. Figure 1 shows the cell structure, current density distribution and Li concentration distribution in AM. The current density near LCO collector became lower because of the lack of Li ion caused by binder migration. In addition, the simulation results were compared with experimental results which were obtained by coin cell with same electrode structure. These results were almost equal to each other. Furthermore, the effect of volume expansion was included in this previous model. Especially, the effect of electrolyte convection caused by the change of porosity was examined. Figure 2 shows the simulation results of the discharge-distribution with volume expansion. From this result, it was found that the effect of convention cannot be ignored in the case of high expansion volume ratio (more than e=2) 
 References 
 [1] G. Inoue et al., Proc. PRiME 2012, B6 #803 (2012) 
 [2] G. Inoue et al., 
 J. Power Source 
 175, 145-158 (2008) 
 [3] G. Inoue et al., ECS Trans., 
 25 (1) 1519-1527 (2009) 
 [4] G. Inoue et al., ECS 226th meeting, A5 #374 (2014)
 
 
 
 
 
 
 Figure 1

results.title: Investigations on the Initial-Stages of Lithium Deposition/Dissolution Processes in Sulfolane Based Electrolytes
results.citation: Ivan Genov et al. (2023) Investigations on the Initial-Stages of Lithium Deposition/Dissolution Processes in Sulfolane Based Electrolytes doi:10.1149/ma2023-025833mtgabs
results.abstract:
Li metal could be the ideal anode for rechargeable battery technologies, due to its negative redox potential (ca. -3 V vs. SHE), high specific capacity (3860 mAh g−1), and low density (0.534 g cm−3) [1-3]. Recent advances show a new design concept where there are no initial active materials in the anode (no carbon, Si, or Li metal) and the corresponding Li quantity is directly deposited on the current collector during charging. This approach will result in important practical advantages such as enhanced volumetric and gravimetric specific energies, ease of manufacturing and reduced complexity/safety concerns of the recycling process (ideally Li is completely removed in the discharged state). However, the applicability of the Li-metal batteries is constrained by the nonuniform lithium deposition, accompanied by dendrite growth and the formation of dead lithium during long-term cycling, which lead to low Coulombic efficiency and even cell failure [2]. The initial structure and morphology of the Li deposit has a vital influence on the later progression of the Li layer [4]. This underpins the importance of a fundamental understanding of the electrodeposition process. However, the presence of a solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) makes such investigations difficult, since after the transport of the Li ions through the SEI, a subsequent charge transfer and nucleation growth process at the substrate-SEI interface, instead of substrate-liquid electrolyte interface occurs. This contribution will discuss lithium electrodeposition in a sulfolane based, localized high concentrated electrolyte system. Possible phase formation mechanisms as well as kinetic and thermodynamic aspects during the initial stages of the process are studied by classical electrochemical methods (Fig. 1, left). Furthermore, the mass-charge balance during deposition and stripping was monitored (Fig. 1, right) via in-situ microgravimetry (electrochemical quartz crystal microbalance). In the contribution we will discuss these transients in terms of lithium layer growth and SEI formation.
 
 References:
 
 [1] X. Cheng et al., (2017) Chemical Reviews, 117 (15), p. 10403-10473.
 [2] J. Cui et al., (2017) Chinese Chemical Letters, 28 (12), p. 2171-2179.
 [3] Z. Hu et al., (2020) Frontiers in chemistry 8, p. 409.
 [4] A. Pei et al., (2017) Nano Letters, 17 (2), p. 1132–1139.
 
 
 
 
 
 Figure 1
